mappings.rationaleAI: Gamete dimorphism as the defining biological trait of sex (B), genetic diversity through recombination (B.3), origin of sex and sexuality (B.4), sex patterns including hermaphroditism (B.5), and chromosomal sex determination systems including XY, ZW, and XO (B.6.a).

Comprehensive coverage of asexual and sexual reproduction strategies (A.2), levels of reproduction from molecular to organismal (A.1), and evolutionary dimensions including the two-fold cost of sex and r/K strategies (A.3).
Flowers as the reproductive structures of angiosperms with stamens and gynoecia (C.3.b), facilitating pollination, fertilization, and seed formation within seed plant reproductive systems.
Natural selection as the mechanism driving the evolution of reproduction and variation control (A.3), including sexual selection as a recognized subtype.
Genetic transmission through reproduction at the molecular level (A.1.a) and the adaptive significance of sex in establishing genetic diversity through recombination (B.3).
Darwin's theory of natural selection and sexual selection directly addresses the evolution of reproduction and variation control (A.3), including The Descent of Man's treatment of sexual selection.
Fruits as seed-bearing structures formed from ovaries post-fertilization in angiosperms, directly part of seed plant reproductive systems (C.3.b).
Organisms as the functional units characterized by autonomous reproduction (A.1.c), connecting the concept of individual organismality to reproduction at the organism level.

The egg as a reproductive vessel and product of animal gonads (D.3.a), with yolk stores and extraembryonic membranes providing for the developing embryo (D.3.d), and egg activation through fertilization or parthenogenesis (D.1).
Reproduction as one of the defining characteristics of life, relevant to molecular replication and cellular reproduction at fundamental levels (A.1).
The distinction between sex, sexuality, and reproduction (B.1), with gender representing the social and psychological dimensions distinguished from biological sex.
Genes as the heritable units transmitted through reproduction at the molecular level (A.1.a) and allelic variation at gene loci relevant to chromosomal sex determination (B.6.a).
Seeds as reproductive units of spermatophytes formed from fertilized ovules, part of seed plant reproductive variation (C.3.b).
The biological, psychological, and sociocultural dimensions of sexual experience relate to distinctions between sex, sexuality, and reproduction (B.1), and hormonal influences on sexuality (B.6.d).
Enduring patterns of attraction as a dimension of sexuality distinct from reproduction (B.1), with biological determinants including prenatal hormonal environment (B.6.d).
Mendelian genetics and molecular mechanisms underlying molecular replication and reproduction (A.1.a), and chromosomal sex determination through sex chromosomes (B.6.a).
Bacterial reproduction through binary fission (A.1.b) and genetic exchange via horizontal gene transfer as a sexlike recombination process (B.2).
Transduction and transformation in viruses as sexlike genetic recombination processes (B.2).
Intrauterine gestation as the provision for the developing embryo in vertebrates (D.3.d), including placental development and maternal physiological adaptations.

Hormonal influences on sex determination (B.6.d), the physiology of plant reproduction including internal factors affecting maturation (C.5), and the role of gonads in hormone cycles in vertebrates (D.3.c).
Broad coverage of animal reproductive systems (D) and life cycles of animals including reproductive strategies (A.1.d).
Plant reproductive systems including sexual and asexual reproduction (C) and plant life cycles with alternation of generations (A.1.d).
Cell reproduction through binary and multiple fission (A.1.b), including somatic versus gamete cell distinction relevant to reproduction.
Archaeal cell reproduction as an example of prokaryotic binary fission (A.1.b).
